cot_prefix: Okay, so I have to figure out which of the two options is sarcastic. The two options are: (A) "Stop putting people in neat little compartments to protect your view point — good idea" (B) "Keep putting people in neat little compartments to protect your view point — good idea" Alright, let's start by understanding what sarcasm is. Sarcasm is a form of irony where someone says the opposite of what they mean, often in a mocking or scornful way. It's usually used to convey something different from what's literally being said, often to criticize or mock. Now, looking at these two statements, they both have two parts: an action and a comment on that action ("good idea"). The difference is the action: one says to "Stop" doing something, the other says to "Keep" doing it. The something is "putting people in neat little compartments to protect your viewpoint." First, I need to understand what "putting people in neat little compartments" means. This is a common metaphor for categorizing or labeling people in a simplistic way, often to maintain one's own perspective or biases.
